relationship_to_restored_text: Interpretive contemplative layer aligned by verse. This does not replace the source-audited restored translation.

verses.mystical_translation: An unidentified body in a field becomes the community's unfinished responsibility.
Elders and judges must leave the gate and measure their nearness to the death.
The nearest city cannot declare itself uninvolved without ritual accountability.
An unworked heifer is taken into an uncultivated valley and killed.
Priests stand beside civic leaders where blood and judgment meet.
The elders wash their hands over the broken life.
They testify that they neither shed nor witnessed the blood.
They ask that innocent blood not remain lodged within the people.
The community must actively clear the claim of unaddressed blood.
The law then turns from the slain stranger to the woman captured in war.
A soldier's desire falls upon someone whose freedom has already been taken.
She is brought into his house and her captive identity is ritually altered.
She receives one month to grieve parents, people, and lost world before the imposed marriage.
If he rejects her, she must go free and may not be sold, because he has harmed her.
A divided household can turn parental desire into inheritance injustice.
The loved wife's son may not displace the actual firstborn.
The disfavored woman's child retains his legal portion.
The chapter next imagines a child defined by parental accusation as stubborn and rebellious.
Father and mother carry him into public judgment.
His life is reduced to labels of excess and refusal.
The ancient law orders the community to kill the child.
Even an executed body may be exposed only briefly.
Burial before nightfall limits humiliation and protects the land from displayed death.

contemplative_note: Deuteronomy 21 moves among communal guilt, captive vulnerability, family favoritism, child execution, and corpse dignity. It cannot be made gentle. Its modern reception must protect the very people most endangered by the ancient structures: captives, women, children, disfavored heirs, and the dead.
